Understanding Transformer Architecture: A Deep Dive

By Author Name January 15, 2024

The transformer architecture has fundamentally changed how we approach natural language processing tasks. In this article, we explore the key innovations that make transformers so effective at modeling sequential data.

Self-Attention Mechanism

At the heart of every transformer is the self-attention mechanism. Unlike recurrent neural networks, self-attention allows the model to look at all positions in the input sequence simultaneously, computing attention weights that determine how much focus to place on each position.

The mathematical formulation involves computing queries, keys, and values from the input embeddings, then using scaled dot-product attention to produce the output representation for each position. 


import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.multihead_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim, num_heads)

    def forward(self, x):
        attn_output, attn_weights = self.multihead_attn(x, x, x)
        return attn_output

Multi-Head Attention

Rather than performing a single attention function, multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel. Each head can learn to attend to different types of information, making the representation richer and more expressive.

Attention is all you need — the original paper demonstrated that a pure attention-based architecture could outperform previous state-of-the-art models on machine translation tasks.

Position Encoding

Since transformers process all positions in parallel, they need a way to encode the order of tokens. Positional encodings are added to the input embeddings to give the model information about the relative or absolute position of tokens in the sequence.

The original transformer used sinusoidal functions of different frequencies for positional encoding. More recent approaches like RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) have shown improved performance on longer sequences.

Feed-Forward Networks

Each transformer layer also contains a position-wise feed-forward network. This consists of two linear transformations with a ReLU activation in between, applied independently to each position.

FFN(x) = max(0, xW1 + b1)W2 + b2

Training and Optimization

Training large transformer models requires careful optimization strategies. The learning rate warmup schedule, gradient clipping, and proper initialization are all critical for stable training of deep transformer architectures.

Recent advances in mixed-precision training and gradient checkpointing have made it possible to train increasingly larger models on available hardware.

Key Takeaways

  • Self-attention enables parallel processing of sequential data with global context
  • Multi-head attention captures diverse relationships in the data
  • Positional encodings compensate for the lack of inherent sequence ordering
  • Modern training techniques have dramatically scaled transformer capabilities

Tags: machine-learning, transformers, nlp, deep-learning
